person.prominent_name_reason: Sophia Fowler Gallaudet — "Mother of the American Deaf." Born deaf; student at the American School for the Deaf from 1817, married its principal Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet in 1821. First matron of the Columbia Institution (later Gallaudet University) 1857-1866. Lobbied Congress to establish the National Deaf-Mute College.

bio.bio_blurb: Deaf wife of Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet

HD0411.prominent_name_reason: Rev. Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet (1787-1851) — Yale 1805 (highest honors, age 17); co-founded with Laurent Clerc and Mason Cogswell the first permanent school for deaf students in North America: the American School for the Deaf, Hartford, April 15, 1817. First principal of the school. Thomas Hooker's 6th-generation descendant through Alice Hooker → Alice Howard → Alice Howard Hopkins → Jennett Hopkins Gallaudet.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded American School for the Deaf

cross_connections.display_label: recruited Gallaudet as Worcester's asylum chaplain in 1838
shared her Thomas Hooker descent, unknown to either family
co-wrote their schoolbooks and edited the Connecticut Observer
led the Michigan School for the Deaf for decades
led the Washington State School for the Deaf

bio.title: Reverend
bio.notes: Named for Capt. Thomas Hopkins, his maternal grandfather

narrative_blocks.header: Founded America's first school for the deaf
narrative_blocks.body: He founded the American School for the Deaf in Hartford on April 15, 1817 — the first permanent school for the deaf in the Western Hemisphere. He had traveled to Paris to learn French Sign Language, returning with deaf teacher Laurent Clerc to build the school together.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his own deaf student Sophia Fowler
narrative_blocks.body: Among his first students was Sophia Fowler, born deaf; he married her in 1821 after her graduation. Their son Edward Miner Gallaudet founded what became Gallaudet University in Washington, carrying the family's mission to the national stage.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-authored religious textbooks with Horace Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1830s and 1840s he collaborated with fellow Hooker descendant Rev. Horace Hooker on Scripture Biography for the Young, The Practical Spelling Book, and The School and Family Dictionary. The spelling book was reprinted under both names for years after his death in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: He crossed the ocean to learn the method
narrative_blocks.body: In 1815 Mason Cogswell's circle sent Gallaudet to Europe to learn how the deaf were taught. At the Paris institute of the Abbé Sicard he persuaded Laurent Clerc, a brilliant deaf instructor, to sail back with him. Clerc became the first deaf teacher of the deaf in America.

narrative_blocks.header: His son's college still carries the name
narrative_blocks.body: His youngest son, Edward Miner Gallaudet, carried the family's work to Washington. There he founded the college that became Gallaudet University, the only university in the world built for deaf students. The name his father made now stands over its gates.

career.organization: American School for the Deaf, Hartford

burialCemetery.name: Cedar Hill Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Major Hartford garden cemetery. Contains graves of J.P. Morgan Sr., Mark Twain, and many prominent Hartford families.
